steps.title: Tie evidence to lighting
steps.content: The researchers claim that the spread of electric lighting in the late nineteenth century produced a shift in human behavior. Strong evidence should connect novels from after that period with more activity during hours when electric lighting would matter most: late at night, after natural daylight was gone.

steps.title: Keep the timing aligned
steps.content: Because the study analyzes time references across novels from many periods, the useful finding must show a before-and-after pattern. A simple frequency comparison is not enough unless it lines up with the late nineteenth-century spread of electric lighting.

steps.title: Eliminate weak patterns
steps.content:
• Choice A concerns 10 a.m., a daytime reference that does not connect to electric lighting.
  • Choice C compares implied time references with clock phrases, so it is about wording patterns rather than behavior.
  • Choice D compares noon and midnight without showing a historical change.

steps.title: Look for late-night change
steps.content: Choice B identifies significantly more references to activities after 10 p.m. in novels published after 1880 than in earlier novels. That timing and late-night focus directly support the conclusion that electric lighting changed human behavior. The answer is B.
